item.title: Down the gVisor Rabbit Hole: A Sunday Afternoon Deep Dive
item.description:
It’s a quiet Sunday afternoon. The kind of afternoon where you have a hot cup of cha on the desk, a terminal window open, and the headspace to write a new post—especially now that I’m starting to make some real headway on a new CRI-O feature. This journey actually began when I decided to get back into open-source software (OSS) following a break after the birth of my daughter.

I reached out to Peter Hunt (@haircommander), one of the lead maintainers of CRI-O, to see if there were any interesting challenges or areas that needed some love. He pointed me toward a long-standing integration puzzle: getting gVisor to play nicely with CRI-O, specifically around how the runtime handles multi-container pods.

item.title: Investigating CRI-O's cmux usage: a code-reading journey
item.description:
I’ve been reading through the CRI-O codebase lately — partly out of curiosity, partly to solidify my mental model of how container runtimes wire themselves together. While doing that I tripped over a piece of plumbing that caught my attention: cmux.

What I found in main.go

While stepping through cmd/crio/main.go, I noticed CRI-O uses soheilhy/cmux — a connection multiplexer — to share a single Unix socket between gRPC and HTTP traffic.

item.title: About
item.description:
Sourav Moitra

I am a Software Engineer with over 8 years of professional experience delivering end-to-end web applications, scalable backend systems, and cloud-native solutions.

I have a deep love for Linux, and the Kubernetes ecosystem. My ultimate goal is to transition into a role where I am contributing directly to Kubernetes core and its surrounding cloud-native landscape.

🚀 What I Do

  • Backend: Expert in Go (Golang) and C.
  • Infrastructure: KCNA & GCP ACE Certified. Strong focus on Kubernetes, CRI-O, and crun.
  • Open Source: Active contributor to CRI-O and crun; aspiring Kubernetes core contributor.

🛠 Experience

  • Lead Engineer @ Capgemini: Developing asynchronous microservices at scale.
  • Senior Software Engineer @ AirAsia MOVE: Led full-stack development for hotel management systems and microservices.
  • Software Engineer @ Smartdrive Systems: Architected resilient microservices and incident dashboards.
  • Sr. Project Associate @ IIT Kanpur: Developed CBT web applications.
  • Jr. Infrastructure Engineer @ Onit: Focused on infrastructure automation and AWS migration.

🌐 Connect

  • GitHub: xw19
  • LinkedIn: sourav-moitra-ng

item.title: How Kubernetes Actually Runs Your Container: A Deep Dive into CRI-O, conmon, and crun
item.description:
You run kubectl apply -f deployment.yaml. Kubernetes “does its thing.” A container appears. But what actually happened between those two events?

Most explanations stop at “the Kubelet talks to the container runtime.” This one doesn’t.

The Control Plane Path

Before we even get to container runtimes, the request has to travel through the control plane.

kubectl apply -f deployment.yaml
    │
    ▼
API Server  ──────▶  etcd  (desired state is stored)
    ▲
    │ writes spec.nodeName
    │
Scheduler  (picks the best node, writes selection back via API Server)
    ▲
    │ ListWatch (Kubelet polls for pods assigned to its node)
    │
Kubelet

One important correction to how people usually picture this: the Scheduler doesn’t push a response back like an RPC call. Instead, it writes the selected node name into etcd through the API Server. The Kubelet on each node is running a ListWatch — a long-lived streaming watch — and detects when a pod gets assigned to it. The Kubelet pulls; nothing pushes to it.
